title: Cannes Film Festival opens, grappling with AI and Hollywood
description: PARIS: Cannes Film Festival rolls out the red carpets on Tuesday for its annual showcase in France, grappling with the dizzying speed of AI-powered disruption and the absence of major Hollywood studios. In its main competition, a total of 22 films are vying for the prestigious Palme d’Or prize for best film, which was won last year by the highly political Iranian movie “It Was Just an Accident” by Jafar Panahi. But as usual in the build up to the world’s biggest festival, off-screen talking points have dominated the conversation, most notably how to cope with changes wrought by artificial intelligence – and Hollywood’s decision to ghost the event. Cannes director Thierry Fremaux came out strongly against AI and its effect on the industry where job losses are mounting for dubbing artists and translators, while writers and actors fear for their livelihoods. “What is certain... is that here in Cannes, we stand with the artists, we stand with the screenwriters and we stand with everyone in these professions, with actors and voice actors alike,” he told a news conference on Monday. Cannes Festival promises escapism in Hollywood-lite edition He suggested that in the future films could be given labels like those for organic food and wine, and “we will say ‘this film has been made without artificial intelligence’”. Nonetheless, the festival announced Monday that it had signed a multi-year sponsorship deal with social media giant and AI technology investor Meta. Industry fears Mark Zuckerberg-owned Meta is at the heart of a brewing controversy about the latest film from Oscar-winning “Traffic” director Steven Soderbergh, which will premiere in Cannes. Soderbergh partnered with Meta to obtain AI-generated video of late Beatles songwriter John Lennon and his wife Yoko Ono for his documentary “John Lennon: The Last Interview”. The use of AI was central to the 2023 strikes that shut down Hollywood, as actors and writers warned that unchecked technology threatened the industry. Thousands of French actors and filmmakers warned in an open letter in February that AI tools were “plundering” talent across the industry, comparing them to a “devouring hydra”. Soderbergh is a rare Hollywood heavyweight in Cannes this year, with others such as Steven Spielberg and Christopher Nolan – hoped for by organisers – failing to appear on the programme. ‘Come back’ The world’s biggest film festival typically spotlights independent, arthouse cinema while relying on Hollywood to provide a dose of mass-market entertainment. But no major US studio agreed to launch a blockbuster this year, or at the Berlin International Film Festival in February, raising questions about why giants such as Universal, Disney or Warner are dodging European events. “I really hope that the studios come back,” Cannes director Fremaux said Monday, attributing their absence to scheduling issues and industry turmoil. He stressed that American cinema was well represented, with “Paper Tiger” by James Gray starring Adam Driver and Scarlett Johansson, as well as “The Man I Love” by Ira Sachs featuring Rami Malek, in the main competition. There will be no shortage of A-listers on the celeb-heavy red carpets. A late addition to the programme includes a cast reunion to mark the 25th anniversary of “The Fast and the Furious”, with Vin Diesel, Michelle Rodriguez and Jordana Brewster set to appear at a special screening on Wednesday. Plane-mad legend John Travolta will bring some stardust when he unveils his directorial debut, “Propeller One-Way Night Coach”, about a young boy’s journey in the “golden age of aviation”. The festival will open with a screening of French film “The Electric Kiss” before the main competition starts on Wednesday, judged by South Korean director Park Chan-wook and Hollywood heavyweight Demi Moore, among others. “I cannot help but feel a sense of emotion, realising that for the first time a Korean has become the head of the jury,” Park told AFP on Monday in Cannes. “The moment has finally come.”

title: Duet holds workshop on AI tools in academia
description: Dhaka University of Engineering and Technology (DUET), Gazipur, held a workshop on harnessing artificial intelligence in academic result processing and course outcome–programme outcome (CO–PO) mapping on Monday. The event, 'AI Tools and Result Preparation with Course Outcome–Programme Outcome Mapping for Capacity Building in Academia', was organised by DUET's Institutional Quality Assurance Cell (IQAC), according to a press statement. title: UiPath Empowers Enterprises with On-Premises Agentic AI Deployment via Automation Suite
description: UiPath a leader in agentic business orchestration, today announced the availability of agentic AI capabilities on UiPath Automation SuiteTM globally, including India. title: AI labels won’t protect misleading ads, says ASCI new guidelines
description: New Delhi: As brands race to integrate artificial intelligence into advertising, the Advertising Standards Council of India (ASCI) has released guidelines aimed at ensuring synthetically generated content does not mislead consumers. The framework attempts to draw a line between creative enhancement and deceptive manipulation, while placing consumer impact at the centre of regulation. The guidelines define synthetically generated content as advertisements that are “artificially created, modified, or materially altered to appear authentic,” including deepfakes, synthetic spokespersons, AI-generated voices and materially altered imagery. Rather than regulating AI technology itself, the draft focuses on outcomes, specifically whether consumers are being deceived, manipulated, or pushed toward false expectations. The guidelines also noted that they align with the SGI Rules amended on February 10, 2026, while aiming to avoid “consumer label fatigue.” The document has stated that “Using AI is only considered misleading or harmful when it creates unfulfillable expectations, exploits vulnerable populations, depicts unsafe situations, or replicates a real person's likeness without consent.” It has further clarified that all advertisements using AI must continue to comply with the entirety of the ASCI Code. At the core of the draft is a risk-based framework that categorises AI-led advertising into high, medium and low-risk buckets. Brands are expected to assess whether AI use is central to an advertisement’s persuasive messaging, materially influences purchase decisions, or creates a misleading impression about a product’s claims or benefits. Under the “High Risk” category, ASCI has proposed an outright prohibition on advertisements that fabricate endorsements, exaggerate product performance, create fake locations, deploy deepfakes without consent, or generate fictional authority figures such as fake doctors promoting supplements. The draft also highlighted examples such as a travel agency depicting a hotel in a non-existent location or showcasing facilities that do not exist. The guidelines clarify that these violations would remain unacceptable even if an AI label is attached to the content. Speaking to BestMediaInfo earlier, Manisha Kapoor had said, “ASCI has already flagged deepfake advertising as a serious concern, noting that hyper-realistic AI content can mislead consumers and damage reputations, especially when endorsements or claims are fabricated. The core issue is erosion of trust. Once consumers are unable to distinguish between authentic and fake brand communication, the credibility of all messaging is diluted.” Read: AI-made campaigns blur consumer trust; brands must respond faster The draft has stated that high-risk advertisements include “Using AI to generate fictional authority figures with identifiable cues, such as an AI generated fake doctor promoting a supplement, implying medical endorsement/expertise.” The “Medium Risk” category introduces mandatory disclosure requirements. ASCI has said labelling becomes necessary when AI materially shapes consumer understanding or influences buying decisions. This includes the use of virtual influencers, cloned voices, AI-generated product demonstrations, fabricated settings, or showcasing products that do not yet exist. The guidelines also noted that disclosure would be required even when a real person’s likeness or voice is replicated with consent for personalised messaging. The guidelines have stated that “Labelling is mandatory in these cases to help consumers understand the nature of the representation.” Among the examples listed are synthetically generated ambassadors, realistic AI-created events, exaggerated AI-generated sound effects that influence perception of a product’s capabilities, and demonstrations of products that do not currently exist, such as unbuilt housing complexes shown through AI-generated visuals. Sponsored AI-generated product recommendations must also explicitly carry the tag “sponsored by”. Kapoor had also said, “Marketers need to build robust content review processes, set internal guidelines and add AI disclaimers where necessary, in marketing materials. Building internal awareness and aligning legal, marketing and digital teams is critical to respond with both speed and consistency in a high-velocity content environment.” ASCI has exempted routine and low-impact uses of AI from disclosure requirements. These include colour correction, lighting tweaks, ambient music, decorative backgrounds, fantastical elements like dragons or fairies, accessibility tools such as audio descriptions for the visually impaired, and administrative uses such as copy generation. The draft has stated that no label is required for “Routine editing, colour correction, noise reduction, standard blemish removal, and minor lighting tweaks that do not alter the substance or core claims of the ad.” To standardise disclosure practices, ASCI has suggested labels such as “Audio/Video created using AI” and “Audio/Video enhanced using AI”, while also allowing brands to use alternative labels that accurately inform consumers. However, the draft has also clarified that disclosures alone cannot legitimise misleading advertising. The draft further noted that “The use of synthetically generated content in some cases may be considered misleading or objectionable regardless of labels if the end effect is likely to mislead or harm the consumer.”

title: High-Bandwidth Memory Solution for AI Servers
description: A memory module is set to power AI servers with higher speed, lower energy use, and smoother performance for large AI workloads. SK Hynix has begun mass production of its 192GB SOCAMM2 memory module, a low-power DRAM solution designed for next-generation AI servers and high-performance AI computing systems. Built on the company’s sixth-generation 10nm-class (1c) [...]
